summary:
The patient presents with classic symptoms of obstructive jaundice — including progressive jaundice, pruritus, and clay-colored stool due to bilirubin accumulation from blocked bile flow. Imaging shows dilation of both the main pancreatic duct and common bile duct, which suggests an upstream blockage at the ampulla of Vater where these two ducts converge. This pattern strongly supports malignancy involving the head of the pancreas as it commonly causes such obstructions through mass effect or compression rather than benign conditions like gallstones that typically affect only the CBD without significant involvement of the PD. Chronic tobacco use increases risk significantly among older adults presenting with this constellation of signs; thus supporting further investigation into neoplastic etiology over inflammatory diseases.

Final Answer: \boxed{\text{Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas}}

The retrieved literature confirms strong associations between pancreatic adenocarcinoma arising at the head region and features consistent with our case—such as prolonged jaundice, cholestatic liver function abnormalities, imaging-demonstrable biliary tree dilation, presence of chronic cigarette smoke exposure history—and especially emphasizes its high prevalence when combined with specific radiographic patterns seen here.

Based upon all available information—including symptom duration, physical exam clues related to biliary compromise, characteristic imaging results showing dual ductal expansion—the likelihood remains overwhelmingly favorably aligned toward malignant pathology originating within the distal pancrease specifically affecting the head area leading directly towards definitive conclusion regarding primary tumor site location despite absence of direct biopsy confirmation yet still pointing clearly toward cancerous origin based on epidemiological correlation and pathophysiological logic inherent across multiple sources cited during retrieval phase.

I do not believe there exists any plausible alternative explanation matching so many criteria simultaneously while maintaining consistency throughout medical principles governing gastrointestinal disease progression pathways under real-world presentation contexts observed clinically today.

Therefore I conclude confidently:

\boxed{\text{Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas}} 

This finding aligns perfectly with known presentations associated with advanced-stage tumors located proximally along digestive tract anatomy influencing downstream drainage systems via mechanical
